tags: bridgetheatre pottersfieldspark haworthtompkins southwark london southbank queenswalk onetowerbridge greaterlondon glc capital england uk city om1 olympus omsystem copyright building architecture londonboroughofsouthwark theatre theater teatro inside interior room cafe bar foyer
description: Bridge Theatre, London. A 900 seat theatre in Southwark, designed by Haworth Tompkins, and opened in October 2017. Part of the One Tower Bridge development, the theatre is largely underground. The auditorium is adaptable and can be configured in end-stage (as seen here), in the round, traverse-stage formats, and has had great success with promenade productions where the audience and cast mingle on a flat seatless stalls floor with seating in the circles. There is a spacious bar and café area on the entrance level. www.bridgetheatre.co.uk/ London Borough of Southwark, South London, England - Bridge Theatre, Potters Fields Park February 2026

title: Prologue 010 : Servery of The Angel
tags: prologue onerevolutionfortheroad rotherhithe london theangel servery bar counter woodpanelling terrazzofloor samuelsmith georgepaulunwin inquest cashingup candle cashbox fire flames burned dead
description:
At the Newington Licensing Sessions held in December 1871 the licence of The Angel was transferred to George Paul Unwin.¹  The son of a builder, he and his wife, Angelina, remained at The Angel throughout the 1870s.

Tragedy struck the Unwin family in a most terrible manner on April 7th, 1883. From the evidence given by the publican at a subsequent inquest held in the tavern, it was a Saturday night, shortly after closing the pub, as he was counting up the money, his wife went to fetch him a candle so that he could extinguish the gas in the house. She came back with it into the bar parlour and placed it upon the table. She then said she was ready for bed, and George Unwin turned out the gas overhead. His wife then stretched her arm over the candle to reach the cash box, when a fancy shawl on her neck caught fire and, in an instant, she became enveloped in flames. Her husband succeeded in extinguishing the fire in a few moments, but she was very much burned about the arms and shoulders. At the inquest it was stated that medical assistance was not obtained till noon on the following day, and death ensued on the following Thursday. She was 48 years of age.²

title: Hope & Anchor, Islington
tags: pub bar london
description: A pub which opened in 1880, becoming famous as a live music venue in the 1970s originally for pub rock and later punk rock. The Stranglers for example recorded a live album here, named after the pub. Incidentally on the day I visited, a Stranglers cover band was playing that evening. Despite being a Greene King pub, it is still a popular live music venue.

title: The Cock Tavern, Somers Town
tags: pub bar london
description:
Phoenix Road, NW1. 

Certainly what I’d describe as a ‘proper pub’, situated between St Pancras International and Euston stations - handy for a quick drink when changing trains! It’s a pub popular with the local Irish community. Sadly no cask ales here, but prices were reasonable and I found it friendly.

description:
Southwark Playhouse Borough, London. - forced to move from their London Bridge home by the redevelopment of the station, Southwark Playhouse was expected to last for around four years in 2013. In 2025 its two auditoria (the Large & the Little) are still operating, joined by the Southwark Playhouse Elephant in 2023. The plan is still to move this theatre to new premises at London Bridge.

description:
Savoy / ABC / Coronet Cinema, Holloway, London. Opened as the Savoy in 1940, designed by W R Glen. Renamed ABC 1962, taken over and renamed Coronet 1979. Closed 1983. Became a snooker hall and from 1996 a Wetherspoon pub - called the Coronet - with the splendid interior largely restored. Unusually, the Coronet was locally listed as an Asset of Community Value (ACV) in 2016.

title: Coronet Holloway Rd 8708
tags: savoy abc coronet hollowayroad london wrglen pub loraineroad greaterlondon glc capital england uk city om1 olympus om system copyright building architecture londonboroughofislington theatre theater teatro cinema cine kino publichouse inn bar inside interior room auditorium seating stalls circle balcony
description:
Savoy / ABC / Coronet Cinema, Holloway, London. Opened as the Savoy in 1940, designed by W R Glen. Renamed ABC 1962, taken over and renamed Coronet 1979. Closed 1983. Became a snooker hall and from 1996 a Wetherspoon pub - called the Coronet - with the splendid interior largely restored. Unusually, the Coronet was locally listed as an Asset of Community Value (ACV) in 2016. 

London Borough of Islington, North London, England - Savoy / ABC / Coronet Cinema (Pub), Holloway Road / Loraine Road
May 2025

title: London Coliseum 9061
tags: london coliseum stmartinslane frankmatcham listed grade2 eno fms city glc greaterlondon cityofwestminster capital england uk building architecture omsystem om1 copyright theatre theater teatro cinema cine kino bar stage inside interior westminster westend
description:
London Coliseum - the American Bar. Frank Matcham's masterpiece, the London Coliseum, opened on Xmas Eve 1904 as a vast Music Hall for Stoll Theatres. Initially it had a capacity of 3,389, now reduced to a more comfortable 2,360, split over four levels. The style is Roman and is unusually lavish throughout using a lot of marble, above the side boxes on either side of the stage is a large statue of a Charioteer drawn by three lions. The theatre was the first in Europe to have lifts to every floor, and also, together with Selfridges, the first place in London to sell Coca-Cola. From 1961 to 1968 it became a cinema with an enormous Cinerama screen installed in front of the proscenium and boxes. It reverted to live shows when Sadlers Wells Opera  (later English National Opera) took over in 1968. Between 2000 and 2004 a meticulous restoration took place. It is an outstandingly important theatre in London and is grade 2* listed.

City of Westminster, West End, London, England - London Coliseum Theatre, St Martin's Lane
May 2025

title: Bridge Theatre 1802
tags: bridgetheatre pottersfieldspark haworthtompkins southwark london southbank queenswalk onetowerbridge greaterlondon glc capital england uk city om1 olympus omsystem copyright building architecture londonboroughofsouthwark theatre theater teatro inside interior room cafe bar foyer
description: Bridge Theatre, London. A 900 seat theatre in Southwark, designed by Haworth Tompkins, and opened in October 2017. Part of the One Tower Bridge development, the theatre is largely underground. The auditorium is adaptable and can be configured in end-stage (as seen here), in the round, traverse-stage formats, and has had great success with promenade productions where the audience and cast mingle on a flat seatless stalls floor with seating in the circles. There is a spacious bar and café area on the entrance level. www.bridgetheatre.co.uk/ London Borough of Southwark, South London, England - Bridge Theatre, Potters Fields Park February 2026

title: Through the Glass
tags: street streetphotography urban candid blackandwhite monochrome bnw coffee cafe window reflections glass layers isolation solitude people citylife london interior exterior observation quiet cinematic lumux g9ii panasonic leica 425mm f12 lens photolab9 on1
description:
I spent a great day with my friend Andrew in London yesterday, taking photos of these things that ca0ught our eye. I like taking photos with a friend in this way; it’s often interesting how differently individuals see what is around them.

It was the last Sunday before Christmas and London was quite busy, and a tad wet. This black-and-white photograph taken through a coffee shop window. Two seated figures are positioned at a counter inside. Although they are sitting close together they remain separate, each absorbed in their own moment.   

Taken using Panasonic Leica 42.5mm f/1.2 lens on my LUMIX G9II, developed with DxO PhotoLab 9 and stylised using ON1 PhotoRAW MAX 2026.

title: Seen While Seeing
tags: street streetphotography urban candid window glass reflections interior exterior cafe restaurant bar openkitchen people worker service observation voyeurism observer reflection selfreflection layers cinematic colour london lumix g9ii microforthirds mft panasonic leica dg nocticron 425mm f12
description:
Ordinarily, I use a polariser all the time to control reflections, however, I removed my polariser for my walk around London to avoid the half-  to full-stop of light loss. I used a lens hood to keep the optics safe instead, which also helped with eliminating extraneous light. 

Viewed through glass, a restaurant interior reveals the scene within; customers at the bar and staff serving food. The reflections merge me as the observer with the scene, blurring the boundary between outside and in. This makes the image more a study of watching and being watched, with the window becoming a membrane rather than a barrier. The reflection makes explicit what is usually implicit in street photography: the photographer is not invisible, merely displaced. 

Taken using Panasonic Leica 42.5mm f/1.2 lens on my LUMIX G9II, developed with DxO PhotoLab 9 and stylised using ON1 PhotoRAW MAX 2026.

title: Through the Glass II
tags: street photography urban candid people cafe bar window glass reflections neon colour modernlife phones isolation solitude citylife london cinematic layers interior exterior lumux g9ii panasonic leica 425mm f12 lens photolab9 on1
description:
Another shot through a window while walking around London on the last Sunday before Christmas.

Like my last post, the image was shot through a window,  and speaks to contemporary urban solitude. It illustrates how modern social spaces allow physical proximity without interaction. The bar becomes a stage where connection is available but deferred, mediated by screens. The viewer is left with a quiet tension between what the setting implies (conversation, companionship) and what is actually happening (parallel isolation).
    
Taken using Panasonic Leica 42.5mm f/1.2 lens on my LUMIX G9II, developed with DxO PhotoLab 9 and stylised using ON1 PhotoRAW MAX 2026.

title: Java Whiskers Cat Cafe
tags: javawhiskerscatcafe london marylebone cafe jainbow
description: Here's the first collection of photos I took at the cat cafe in Marylebone, London. We had the choice to visiting the kitten room, which was a bit more expensive, or the cat room, which was the one we chose. The cats are all rescued cats, and quite nervous. Apparently there are 12 cats altogether, but it's hard to know whether we saw all of them or just the braver ones! My favourite was the tabby, which was quite playful and reminded me of my Gran's cat, Tibby, from when I was a child.

tags: architecture artscentre cafe cafés pavilions serpentinegallery architectural architecturaldetail artgallery building canopy commercialbuilding edifice edifices glassscreen leisurebuildings recreationbuilding recreationbuildings roof rooves screen structures london england unitedkingdom geo:state=england exif:make=nikon camera:make=nikon exif:model=nikonf6 geo:lat=51504595483333 exif:lens=170350mmf28 geo:location=theserpentinegallerysouthkensingtongardensw23xa exif:isospeed=200 geo:country=unitedkingdom geo:lon=017442805 geo:city=london camera:model=nikonf6
description: Serpentine Pavilion 2025 by Marina Tabassum “A Capsule in Time by Marina Tabassum and her firm, Marina Tabassum Architects (MTA) Celebrated for her work in establishing an architectural language that is both contemporary and deeply connected to a specific place, climate, context, culture, and history, Marina Tabassum brings her distinctive vision to the Serpentine Pavilion 2025. Her design evokes a meaningful dialogue between the permanent and ephemeral nature of the commission. Along the north-south axis of the park, A Capsule in Time features an elongated capsule-like form with a central court that aligns with Serpentine South’s bell tower. Inspired by summer park-going and arched garden canopies that filter soft daylight through green foliage, the structure is comprised of four wooden sculptural forms with a translucent façade that diffuses and dapples light when it enters the space. Integral to Tabassum’s design is a kinetic element where one of the capsule forms is able to move, connect and transform the Pavilion into a new space. Emphasising the sensory and spiritual possibilities of architecture through scale and the interplay of light and shadow, Tabassum’s design draws on the history and architectural tradition of Shamiyana tents or awnings of South Asia. Similarly kinetic in their function, these structures are made of fabric supported by bamboo poles and are commonly erected for outdoor gatherings and celebrations. The openness of Tabassum’s Pavilion welcomes the possibilities of unifying visitors through conversations, connections, live programmes and public convenings.” All text above copyright the Serpentine Gallery, 2025. Cited from: www.serpentinegalleries.org/whats-on/serpentine-pavilion-...

title: Barbarista
tags: shop barber bar cafe barbershop coffeeshop cute authentic creative street lane alley passage camdenpassage angel islington london england britain uk front sign signage logo ottoman door window interior light warm welcome welcoming man lad sitting reading browsing pricelist services design designer culture lifestyle streetlife trade service mobile phone smartphone iphone 16pro pi projectindigo photoshop acr cameraraw lightroom
description:
'Otto Man' sounds like a German brand: in the logo, at least - otherwise it's obviously 'the Ottoman' (Turk).

Turkish barber shops have mushroomed all over London (and the country). Some say they are 'laundromats' (a front for 'other' business) but, to be honest, most provide an excellent service and pleasant experience. Even the most humble ones, and this one's a bit special, as befits the picturesque Camden Passage.
And - while all offer courtesy Turkish tea, this one has a proper Espresso bar: a dual-purpose business, as the sign says ;)

title: Battersea Arts Centre 4520
tags: batterseaarts claphamjunction lavenderhill theatrestreet townhallroad townhall listed grade2 victorian edwardmountford haworthtompkins wandsworth bac greaterlondon glc capital england uk city om1 olympus omsystem copyright building architecture londonboroughofwandsworth theatre theater teatro cinema cine kino concerthall artscentre inside interior room cafe
description:
Battersea Arts Centre, Clapham Junction. Located on the ground floor, the Café Bar also hosts live events, from music, to monthly quizzes. Opened as the Town Hall in 1893, designed by E W Mountford, and converted into an arts centre in 1973. Grade 2* listed, it suffered a major fire at the rear of the building which destroyed the Grand Hall, and caused heat and smoke damage to other parts. Occurring on Friday 13th March 2015, parts of the building were able to reopen within a day, but the restoration of the Grand Hall took until 2018 to complete, overseen by Haworth Tompkins Architects. Much of the rebuild was finished in a 'distressed' state, rather than restoring the original decor. The Complex contains three theatres, bars, a café, and a magnificent entrance foyer. It hosts theatre, exhibitions, conferences and weddings.

London Borough of Wandsworth, South London, England - Battersea Arts Centre (BAC), Lavender Hill / Town Hall Road / Theatre Street
August 2024

tags: pub restaurant bar tavern cafe interior salon hall light shadows ornaments decor arches chandeliers historic old traditional classic atmosphere mood people customers visitors guests crowd social socialising chatting talking drinking iconic oldbankofengland fleetstreet city cityoflondon squaremile london england britain uk mobile phone cellphone smartphone iphone 14pro photoshop acr
description:
Just a pub, actually, but interior grandeur may infer associations with an imposing historic institution. In fact, the building was, indeed a branch of the BoE for some years, serving the adjacent Royal Courts of Justice - but was later refurbished into this remarkable pub.

Snapped separate shots with the ultrawide and the main cameras, the latter showing better detail, but the wide one showing the ambience better (imho)...

description: Was serving Cuban and Latin American cuisine in Smithfield, London. It has the date of 1878 in the masonry and I suspect the big glass doors would have been the entrance for horses to go through, probably for stables. Checking their website shows the area is up for redevelopment and the restaurant is now closed. Hope the building doesn't get demolished. London EC1 Taken with my digital Fujifilm X20

title: Charles Dickens Museum - Sculpture behind the cafe
tags: londonboroughofcamden london england unitedkingdom greatbritain bloomsbury doughtyst 4849doughtyst charlesdickensmuseum charlesdickens gradeilisted gradeilistedbuilding charlesdickenshouseandattachedrailings terracedhouse darkenedstockbrickwithslatemansardroofanddormer plainstucco sculpture cafe dickensstudy chair chairs
description: A visit to the Charles Dickens Museum at 48-49 Doughty Street in Bloomsbury, London. Dickens lived here with his wife Catherine and family from 1837, for three years. By 1839 he had become a superstar having written The Pickwick Papers, Oliver Twist and Nicholas Nickleby. The house at 48 Doughty Street was a first class family mansion, with a basement, a ground, first, second and third floor. The Charles Dickens Museum is an author's house museum at 48 Doughty Street in King's Cross, in the London Borough of Camden. It occupies a typical Georgian terraced house which was Charles Dickens's home from 25 March 1837 (a year after his marriage) to December 1839. In the nineteenth century, it was an exclusive residential street and had gates at either end to restrict entry and these were manned by porters. Charles Dickens and his wife Catherine Dickens (née Hogarth) lived here with the eldest three of their ten children, with the older two of Dickens's daughters, Mary Dickens and Kate Macready Dickens being born in the house. A new addition to the household was Dickens's younger brother Frederick. Also, Catherine's 17-year-old sister Mary moved with them from Furnival's Inn to offer support to her married sister and brother. It was not unusual for a woman's unwed sister to live with and help a newly married couple. Dickens became very attached to Mary, and she died in his arms after a brief illness in 1837. She inspired characters in many of his books, and her death is fictionalized as the death of Little Nell. Dickens had a three-year lease (at £80 a year) on the property. He would remain here until 1839 when he moved to Devonshire Terrace. He upscaled to grander homes as his wealth increased and his family grew. However, Doughty Street is his only surviving London house. The two years that Dickens lived in the house were extremely productive, for here he completed The Pickwick Papers (1836), wrote the whole of Oliver Twist (1838) and Nicholas Nickleby (1838–39) and worked on Barnaby Rudge (1840–41). The building at 48 Doughty Street was threatened with demolition in 1923, but was saved by the Dickens Fellowship, founded in 1902, who raised the mortgage and bought the property's freehold. The house was renovated and the Dickens House Museum was opened in 1925, under the direction of an independent trust, now a registered charity. The house was listed in 1954. Perhaps the best-known exhibit is the portrait of Dickens known as Dickens's Dream by R. W. Buss, an original illustrator of The Pickwick Papers. This unfinished portrait shows Dickens in his study at Gads Hill Place surrounded by many of the characters he had created. The painting was begun in 1870 after Dickens's death. Other notable artefacts in the museum include numerous first editions, original manuscripts, original letters by Dickens, and many personal items owned by Dickens and his family. The only known item of clothing worn by Dickens still in existence is also displayed at the museum. This is his Court Suit and sword, worn when Dickens was presented to the Prince of Wales in 1870. Grade I listed building Charles Dickens House and Attached Railings Sculpture behind the café From the first floor window in Dickens' study. Had to lift up the blind to take this one.

description:
If ever I am near Paddington Station , West London and feeling hungry . I beat a path to Mickey's Fish & Chips , situated in Norfolk Place , off of Praed Street . 

Always excellent value , cooked to order . 

Fish , Chips , Baked Beans ( or peas ) Bread & Butter and a Can of Pop , what else do you need ! ( No they don't do curry sauce ) . 

Late Monday afternoon 25th-July-2022 . 

( Inspite of what Flickr Maps think.... It is no where near Hyde Park , London ) !

title: 157 - London 8
tags: london cafe woman olympus21mm
description:
Opening up the UK had been made more difficult by the emergence and increasingly rapid spread of the 'India' variant, but the Government opened up fully on 19 July - all precautions ditched, which is provoking increasing disbelief among many and conflicting policy differences in other UK countries.. Vaccinations are being pushed to cover both younger adults and those needing their second shot. Meanwhile, providing vaccines to poorer countries and cutting international aid remain topical.

 Since the March 2020 lock-down for Covid I have taken the opportunity to upload shots from before September 2010. In-between contemporary shots, I am uploading photos pre-SPNC, from the days of my old Olympus E-1/E-330/420. Not many are any good but the exercise has given me an opportunity to look back. One thing that has struck me is how limiting the effect of noise was when trying to take (very early) street shots at ISO 800 in relatively dark conditions. I still used zoom lenses quite a lot until SPNC and am glad to have been educated in the use of a wider angle lens.

title: Interior of the Mary Ward Centre café, Queen Square
tags: london bloomsbury queensquare building city urban street marywardcentre interior cafe
description: The Mary Ward Centre café - an interior view looking outside onto Queen Square, Bloomsbury, London. Nice and peaceful before the lunchtime rush ;) The Mary Ward Centre is part of the Mary Ward Settlement, in Queen Square, London. It was founded by Mary Augusta Ward, a Victorian novelist and founding president of the Women's National Anti-Suffrage League, better known by her married name Mrs Humphrey Ward. It is located in Queen Square, Bloomsbury, an area of central London known for its literary and educational heritage. description: Beppe’s Café was established in 1932 by the Papini family in West Smithfield, London, and still going strong – “the locals relish this family-run place with its sense of local history and legendary frosty service”, says the Classic Cafés website. “It's always busy, full of hospital workers from Bart’s opposite. description: Beppe’s Café was established in 1932 by the Papini family in West Smithfield, London, and still going strong – “the locals relish this family-run place with its sense of local history and legendary frosty service”, says the Classic Cafés website. “It's always busy, full of hospital workers from Bart’s opposite. It looks uncomfortably small but inside there are just enough booths to be able to snatch some space. Beppe's is quite simply one of the pleasantest places to while away a lunchtime.”

title: Pictures at an Exhibition
tags: nationalportraitgallery london art gallery exhibition painting npg
description:
Visited the National Portrait Gallery in London with a friend. Been there many times, and it was really just a place to chill and chat. It has free entry, which I really value. It's good for the soul, and I'd be more than happy for a small city tax levied on hotel stays to help fund these galleries.

National Portrait Gallery, London WC2
14th March 2026

title: Wood Carving - Serpentine Gallery - Summer 25
tags: london england unitedkingdom hyde park serpentine gallery art wood carving summer july 2025 leica knightsbridge belgravia westminster
description:
"The Serpentine Galleries are two contemporary art galleries in Kensington Gardens, Westminster, Greater London. Recently rebranded to just Serpentine, the organisation is split across Serpentine South (previously known as the Serpentine Gallery) and Serpentine North (previously known as the Sackler Gallery).

The gallery spaces are within five minutes' walk of each other, linked by the bridge over the Serpentine Lake from which the galleries get their names. Their exhibitions, architecture, education and public programmes attract up to 1.2 million visitors a year. Admission to both galleries is free. The CEO is Bettina Korek, and the artistic director Hans Ulrich Obrist." - Wikipedia

title: The Family of Henry VIII - English School
tags: family school english henry englishschool thefamilyofhenryviii london art palace buckinghampalace buckingham artgalleryandmuseums gallery kings thekingsgallery
description: This painting was probably made for Whitehall Palace. Henry VIII is flanked by Jane Seymour and Prince Edward. The princesses Mary and Elizabeth stand in the side panels. The figures through the arches may be Jane 'the Foole' and William Somer. They were entertainers and jesters to the court. On show in the Kings Gallery, Buckingham Palace, London. www.rct.uk/visit/the-kings-gallery-buckingham-palace

title: Letizia Bettaglia Exhibition at the Photographers’ Gallery, London
tags: cafes photographers’ gallery london
description: As a member of the Photographers’ Gallery (on Ramiles St. just of New Oxford St. London) I can visit as often as I like for my annual subscription. Throughout the year it puts on some really excellent exhibitions of really excellent work. This exhibition of Letizia’s work was just fabulous. On two floors it captures the essence of her work around Sicily. The title of the exhibition is “Life, Love and Death in Sicily”. I took this picture of part of the exhibition and was attracted by the light from the window and the reflection. You can find out more about this lady on wikipedia…the link is… en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letizia_Battaglia?wprov=sfti1# Also note you don’t have to be a member but there is a small fee to pay to visit the gallery.

title: The Sea at L'Estaque - Paul Cézanne
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums nationalgallery london national gallery theseaatlestaque paulcézanne the sea lestaque paul cézanne
description:
On display in the National Gallery, London.

In the spring of 1876, Cézanne was staying in L'Estaque, a fishing village near Marseilles. He worked out of doors, focussing on the violent Mediterranean light and its ability to both enhance colours and flatten volumes.

title: Miss La La at the Cirque Fernando - Edgar Degas
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums nationalgallery london national gallery misslalaatthecirquefernando edgardegas cirque fernando edgar degas lala
description:
On display in the National Gallery, London.

The acrobat Miss La La caused a sensation at the Cirque in Paris. Degas paints her suspended from the rafters of the circus by a rope clenched between her teeth.

title: Landscape with Poplars - Paul Cézanne
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums nationalgallery london national gallery landscapewithpoplars paulcézanne landscape poplars paul cézanne
description:
On display in the National Gallery, London.

The landscape is that of Cézanne's native Provence. Cézanne uses a variety of hatched brushstrokes to unify the meadow, trees and architecture.

title: The Thames below Westminster - Claude Monet
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums nationalgallery london national gallery thethamesbelowwestminster claudemonet thames westminster claude monet
description:
On display in the National Gallery, London.

The grey hazy sky successfully evokes the fogs for which 19th century London was notorious. This was painted around 1871 while Monet was avoiding the Franco-Prussian war.

title: Daunt's bookshop, London
tags: bookshop gallery dauntbooks marylebone london england europe europa jamesdaunt edwardian architecture books literature book livre buch knjiga kniha bog boek bok raamat kirja libro livro llyfr ©peterdenton samsunggalaxys24
description: Galleries and galleries of books, books, books! This is another view of Daunt’s in Marylebone – surely the most beautiful bookshop in London. Originally an antiquarian book emporium which was completed in 1912, it was bought by James Daunt in 1990. And while it’s primarily a travel bookshop, its range of other subjects and titles is now extensive. Known to everyone as 'Daunts', visiting this lovely place is an experience and a delight. ♦ While you’re here… I have two Galleries that might interest you: a Bookshops gallery and a Public Libraries gallery. Happy browsing!

title: Daunts: London's most beautiful bookshop

description: Daunt Books in Marylebone must surely be the most beautiful bookshop in London. It’s an original Edwardian emporium comprising long oak galleries and large skylights, and is a delight to visit. Originally an antiquarian bookshop, the premises were completed in 1912. It was bought by James Daunt in 1990 and while it’s primarily a travel bookshop its range of other subjects is now extensive. Known to everyone as 'Daunts', visiting this lovely place is an experience to be savoured. ♦ While you’re here… I have two Galleries that might interest you: a Bookshops gallery and a Public Libraries gallery. Happy browsing!

description: Daunt Books in Marylebone must surely be the most beautiful bookshop in London. It’s an original Edwardian emporium comprising long oak galleries and large skylights, and is a delight to visit. Originally an antiquarian bookshop, the premises were completed in 1912. It was bought by James Daunt in 1990 and while it’s primarily a travel bookshop its range of other subjects is now extensive. title: UK - London Flickr Photowalk 15 - Royal Academy - Marina Abramovic_5003910
tags: uklondonflickrphotowalk15royalacademymarinaabramovic5003910 marinaabramović marinaabramovic royalacademy ra gallery museum burlingtonhouse picadilly contemporaryart modernart art artist sign advert britain building england greatbritain nikon omot travel travelling travelphotographer travelphotography unitedkingdom wwwdgphotoscouk ©dgodliman
description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd During December's London Flickr Festive Photowalk we passed through the Royal Academy. This large Marina Abramović advert in the front courtyard of Burlington House caught my eye thanks to the range of expressive faces she's making. Click here to see more photos from this and previous London Flickr Group photowalks : www.flickr.com/photos/darrellg/albums/72177720301569918 From Wikipedia, "Marina Abramović (born November 30, 1946) is a Serbian conceptual and performance artist. Her work explores body art, endurance art, the relationship between the performer and audience, the limits of the body, and the possibilities of the mind. Being active for over four decades, Abramović refers to herself as the "grandmother of performance art". She pioneered a new notion of identity by bringing in the participation of observers, focusing on "confronting pain, blood, and physical limits of the body". In 2007, she founded the Marina Abramović Institute (MAI), a non-profit foundation for performance art." © D.Godliman

title: Rushing for knowledge (British Museum, London, United Kingdom)
tags: london uk british museum great hall andreapucci
description:
United Kingdom, London, British Museum

After moving the British Library to St. Pancras, the central quadrangle of the British Museum in London was redeveloped to become the Queen Elizabeth II Great Court, commonly referred to simply as the Great Court. It was opened by Queen Elizabeth II in 2000. The court has a tessellated glass roof covering the entire court and surrounds the original circular British Museum Reading Room in the centre, now a museum. It is the largest covered square in Europe. The glass and steel roof is made up of 4,878 unique steel members connected at 1,566 unique nodes and 1,656 pairs of glass windowpanes, making up 6,100m2 of glazing; each of a unique shape because of the undulating nature of the roof.

title: Visit to London - February 2023
tags: london city hoxton people museum wall home strand
description: I’m sometimes a bit slow in sharing pictures I’ve taken on my visits to various places. (Frankly, that’s a massive understatement!) This album contains pictures of the city of London, the Museum of London, the Museum of the Home in Hoxton, a London suburb and other shots around our walk from the city out to Hoxton. And then some more on our journey back into London.

title: Dennis Severs' house on Folgate Street.
tags: 2023 england europe folgatestreet fujixt30ii fujifilmxt30ii greatbritain london spitalfields uk xf1855mm house museum
description: This house is situated on Folgate Street, in the Spitalfields area of London. It once belonged to an eccentric American called Dennis Severs. Dennis spent much of his time at the local antique stalls and bought period items for his home. He wanted to make it look like it would have done, when originally built in 1724. He passed away some time ago, however the house is open to visitors (if they book a tour). We did do the tour - it's impressive and pretty odd! Taken with my Fujifilm X-T30 II camera and a XF18-55mm lens.

title: Picasso, his work and his audience - Pablo Picasso
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums britishmuseum british museum picassohisworkandhisaudience pablopicasso work audience pablo picasso
description: In autumn 2024, the British Museum had an exhibition of Picasso prints, drawings and etchings. This print, set in a circus, includes a self-portrait with wispy hair and wrinkles. Many sets of eyes look out from the dark, eager for the spectacle to unfold. www.britishmuseum.org/exhibitions/picasso-printmaker

title: The Three Dancers by Picasso - Tate Modern - January 2026

title: The little artist - Pablo Picasso
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums british museum britishmuseum thelittleartist pablopicasso little artist pablo picasso
description: In autumn 2024, the British Museum had an exhibition of Picasso prints, drawings and etchings. in 1953, Françoise Gilot left Picasso, taking their children with her. His sadness is evident in his depiction of this scene in which the children draw and play while their mother watches over them protectively. www.britishmuseum.org/exhibitions/picasso-printmaker

title: Still life. Fruit bowl - Pablo Picasso
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums british museum britishmuseum stilllifefruitbowl pablopicasso still fruit bowl picasso pablo life
description: In autumn 2024, the British Museum had an exhibition of Picasso prints, drawings and etchings. This is one of Picasso's earliest Cubist prints, made in 1908/09. Taking a simple still life, he plays with space and viewpoint, presenting visual information in a single frame that would normally be gathered from multiple angles. www.britishmuseum.org/exhibitions/picasso-printmaker

title: Raphael and La Fornarina I - Pablo Picasso
tags: art artgalleryandmuseums british museum britishmuseum raphaelandlafornarinai pablopicasso raphael fornarina pablo picasso i
description: In autumn 2024, the British Museum had an exhibition of Picasso prints, drawings and etchings. Picasso's 25 etchings on the theme of Raphael and La Fornarina (the baker's daughter) depict the lovers repeatedly having sex or lying together in the artist's studio. Picasso was inspired by Japanese erotic woodblock prints, known as shunga, which he collected. They date from 1968. www.britishmuseum.org/exhibitions/picasso-printmaker

description: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy), 1889 The Cleveland Museum of Art, Gift of the Hanna Fund, 1947.209.  Like Two Poplars in the Alpilles near Saint-Rémy (on view nearby), Vincent van Gogh painted this autumnal landscape while living at an asylum near Saint-Rémy in southern France where he was treated for severe depression. Understanding that painting from nature eased his symptoms, Van Gogh’s physician permitted the artist to paint landscapes outside of the asylum. Van Gogh described this painting in a letter to his brother Theo: “The last study I did is a view of the village, where they were at work under some enormous plane trees—repairing the pavements. . . . There are heaps of sand, stones, and the gigantic trunks—the leaves yellowing and here and there you can get a glimpse of a house front and small figures.”

title: The Lover (Portrait of Lieutenant Milliet),
tags: arles kröllermüllermuseum london nationalgallery vincentvangogh art painting poetsandlovers vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
description:
The Lover (Portrait of Lieutenant Milliet), 1888 Kröller-Müller Museum, Otterlo, The Netherlands. Painting and drinking companion
Paul-Eugène Milliet is a second lieutenant in a Zouave regiment of the French army (this is indicated by the moon and star in the top right corner). In Arles he is Vincent’s painting and drinking companion. He takes drawing lessons from the artist for some time and accompanies him on his painting trips.

Prototype of a lover
Van Gogh, whose contact with women is awkward, admires the lieutenant for his amorous escapades. With some self-irony he confesses to Theo: ‘Milliet’s lucky, he has all the Arlésiennes he wants, but there you are, he can’t paint them, and if he was a painter he wouldn’t have any.’ In this portrait he depicts him as the prototype of a lover.

Contrasts
The face is made up of loose, thin brushstrokes, while the background is applied with broad, thick strokes. The hat, then again, is painted thinly and evenly. The colours are also contrasting. Van Gogh writes that Milliet is not very good at sitting as a model, but that he is satisfied with ‘his face with its pale, matt complexion, the red képi against an emerald background’.

title: The Yellow House (The Street) 1888
tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting vangoghmuseum vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
description:
The Yellow House (The Street) 1888 Van Gogh Museum, Amsterdam (Vincent van Gogh Foundation).  In May 1888, Van Gogh rented four rooms in a house on Place Lamartine in Arles (southern France). The green shutters in this painting of the square show where he lived. Shortly after moving into the ‘Yellow House’, he sent Theo a description and sketch of his painting of it: ‘it’s tremendous, these yellow houses in the sunlight and then the incomparable freshness of the blue.’

The work, which Van Gogh himself called ‘The Street’, records the artist’s immediate surroundings: he often ate at the restaurant on the left, and the home of his friend, the postman Joseph Roulin, lay just beyond the second railway bridge.

Vincent had finally found a place at the Yellow House where he could not only paint but also have his friends come to stay. His plan was to turn the yellow corner-building into an artists’ house, where like-minded painters could live and work together.

title: Sunflowers1888 National Gallery, London, England
tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting sunflowers vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
description:
This is one of five versions of Sunflowers on display in museums and galleries across the world. Van Gogh made the paintings to decorate his house in Arles in readiness for a visit from his friend and fellow artist, Paul Gauguin.

‘The sunflower is mine’, Van Gogh once declared, and it is clear that the flower had various meanings for him. The different stages in the sunflower’s life cycle shown here, from young bud through to maturity and eventual decay, follow in the vanitas tradition of Dutch seventeenth-century flower paintings, which emphasise the transient nature of human actions. The sunflowers were perhaps also intended to be a symbol of friendship and a celebration of the beauty and vitality of nature.

The sunflower pictures were among the first paintings Van Gogh produced in Arles that show his signature expressive style. No other artist has been so closely associated with a specific flower, and these pictures are among Van Gogh’s most iconic and best-loved works.

title: Sunflowers1889 Philadelphia Museum of Art,
tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting philadelphiamuseumofart sunflowers vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
description: Vincent van Gogh painted this luminous image of sunflowers from memory, in the depths of winter in 1889. Throughout his ten-year career, Van Gogh painted sunflowers repeatedly in different arrangements and settings. The shapes, colors, and cheerfulness of the modest flower appealed to him. He associated its yellow color with sunshine, the south, and Christ, the light of the world. Over a single week in August 1888, Van Gogh painted four pictures of sunflowers, including a bold canvas of twelve sunflowers against a turquoise ground, now in the Neue Pinakothek, Munich. The speed of this work was driven by enthusiasm and necessity since the flowers were destined to wilt and fade. This painting is a variation on the work now in Munich. Far from being a simple copy, it is a new interpretation that gives each flower a pronounced personality.

title: Field with Poppies, 1889
tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting kunsthallebremen vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
description: Field with Poppies, 1889 Kunsthalle Bremen-Der Kunstverein in Bremen.  At the beginning of May 1889, four and a half months after his serious breakdown, Vincent van Gogh entered an asylum in Saint- Rémy in the Provence. When he was allowed to leave the grounds of the institution at the beginning of June, Field with Poppies was one of the first motifs he decided to paint. From a slightly elevated vantage point, he depicts the view over the valley with grain fields: It is a tapestry of impastoed colors dominated by the contrasting effect of the complementary colors of red and green. The swirls of color lend the canvas dynamism, whereas the lines of perspective draw the viewer’s gaze into the distance. When Gustav Pauli acquired Field with Poppies for 30,000 marks, thus continuing with his progressive purchasing policy, he sparked an art scandal that has since become famous. The Worpswede painter Carl Vinnen initiated the “Protest of German Artists,” a pamphlet in which numerous artists and art critics came out against the purported “foreign infiltration of German art” and what in their opinion was an increased acquisition of French art by German museums—an accusation that was not borne out by the facts. Several months later, the “Answer to the Protest of German Artists” was published, to which prominent artists of the avant-garde contributed, such as August Macke, Franz Marc, Max Pechstein, and Vassily Kandinsky.

title: Tree Trunks in the Grass, 1890
tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting kröllermüllermuseum vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
description:
Kröller-Müller Museum
Close-up
During his Paris period, Van Gogh already painted a forest in an unusual and original way: not the tall trees and the foliage, but the ground, the base of the trunks and the low vegetation, such as ivy, grass and small plants. During his stay at the asylum in Saint-Rémy he again makes this type of ‘close-up’ of a piece of grassland with flowers and the trunks of two pine trees.

Powerful spatial effect
He paints with thick and thin lines, solid contours and small circles for the many flowers. The rough bark of the pine trees is rendered almost abstractly, while the grass field with white flowers and the yellow dandelions displays a certain sophistication. By emphasizing the foreground – the trees and the grass – and using a strong diagonal, a powerful spatial effect is created.

Untangled
He writes to his friend Emile Bernard: ‘I exaggerate, I sometimes make changes to the subject, but still I don’t invent the whole of the painting; on the contrary, I find it ready-made – but to be untangled – in the real world’.

description: Detail of a painting by Duncan Grant at Tate Britain museum in London, Great-Britain. This was painted in the summer of 1911 as part of a decorative scheme for the dining room at the Borough Politechnic, London. The theme was 'London on Holiday', and Grant responded by painting this idealised panorama of seven nudes bathing. Grant's design was inspired by Michelangelo's male nudes and summers spent at the Serpentine in Hyde Park, which was a site associated with London's gay culture. title: Seen While Seeing
tags: street streetphotography urban candid window glass reflections interior exterior cafe restaurant bar openkitchen people worker service observation voyeurism observer reflection selfreflection layers cinematic colour london lumix g9ii microforthirds mft panasonic leica dg nocticron 425mm f12
description:
Ordinarily, I use a polariser all the time to control reflections, however, I removed my polariser for my walk around London to avoid the half-  to full-stop of light loss. I used a lens hood to keep the optics safe instead, which also helped with eliminating extraneous light. 

Viewed through glass, a restaurant interior reveals the scene within; customers at the bar and staff serving food. The reflections merge me as the observer with the scene, blurring the boundary between outside and in. This makes the image more a study of watching and being watched, with the window becoming a membrane rather than a barrier. The reflection makes explicit what is usually implicit in street photography: the photographer is not invisible, merely displaced. 

Taken using Panasonic Leica 42.5mm f/1.2 lens on my LUMIX G9II, developed with DxO PhotoLab 9 and stylised using ON1 PhotoRAW MAX 2026.

title: BBar, Victoria, London
tags: bbar victoria london cocktailbar restaurant jainbow
description: As part of our anniversary weekend, we decided it would be nice to have an afternoon tea on the Sunday. Afternoon teas can be very expensive and not all the options were open to us on a Sunday, but we found one that was perfect for us - the B Bar! Instead of sandwiches on the bottom shelf - there's usually one sandwich that no one wants! - there was fish and chips! Top shelf up was the traditional scones, clotted cream and strawberry jam. The middle shelf was a selection of desserts. We had to admit defeat after the lovely scones, so the kind waitress gave us a box so we could (carefully!) take the desserts home on the train! It was lovely and I highly recommend it! Located beneath London’s largest living wall, bbar is a vibrant cocktail bar and restaurant in the heart of Victoria, London. Opposite Buckingham Palace and close to Victoria Station, bbar is perfectly positioned, and offers pre-theatre dining for Hamilton and Wicked at nearby theatres. Our menu features signature classics with a modern twist, from fish and chips to our popular 'Son of a Gun' burger. Enjoy delicious signature cocktails on our sunny terrace or catch live sports screenings, all in a family-friendly environment. (From the B Bar website)

description: Their was precious little sunshine over the weekend of the Wife's 50th Birthday. So having done 'Rules' with family on the Saturday we ate at 'The Duck & Waffle' on the 40th Floor of Heron Tower on the Sunday. The River Thames top right can be seen snaking towards Canary Wharf in the distance. I took shots on my mobile and my FZ before the rain began to lash at the windows. Heron Tower is one of the few tall buildings I've not been up in London so eating here meant that I got to tick off another tall building in the City!

description:
What's the purpose of the ubiquitous 'The' in pub names? By what law every single 'Fox and Hound', 'Queen Vic' or 'Wagon and Horses' in the country must absolutely be 'the THE..'??

Can't they phase it out like with rock bands, which were initially all The Beatles, The Kinks, The Who etc - but later became just Led Zeppelin, Yes, or Nirvana - and nobody imagines putting a 'the'in front??

Just pondering

title: Covent Garden Hotel / Brasserie Max.
tags: 2025 coventgardenhotel england fujixt30ii fujifilmxt30ii london uk xf1855mm brasserie flag restaurant
description: On our wanderings through West London, we visited Neal's Yard. On our exit from there, we popped out onto Monmouth Street, right in front of this very upmarket establishment - the Covent Garden Hotel. It was once a French hospital, with the history etched into the brickwork. Room prices start at around £500 a night. Suites are just a touch more! If you have the means, it looks fabulous. Taken with my Fujifilm X-T30 II camera and a XF18-55mm lens.

title: The Ivy and The Mousetrap
tags: street london capitalcity england uk nightscene stmartin’stheatre theivy restaurant themousetrap play whodunit crime theater agathachristie europe europa ©peterdenton canoneos200d sigma18300mm londonwestend coventgarden
description: The West End of London, and two landmarks in the same image – The Ivy restaurant and St Martin’s Theatre, which hosts the most famous play in the world, now in its 73rd year of a non-stop run. The play is, of course, The Mousetrap, which began life as a 20-minute BBC radio play, Three Blind Mice. But the writer, Agatha Christie, expanded it to a full-length stage play which opened in London in 1952. It’s been running ever since, and is the longest-running theatre production anywhere in the world. Before it opened, Christie gave the royalties to her grandson, Matthew Pritchard, without any idea that it would become the phenomenon that it now is. Lucky man! The Ivy restaurant opened in 1917. It has become a theatrical institution and a popular haunt of showbusiness people, so much so that mobile phones and cameras are forbidden in the restaurant. There are now some 30 other Ivy restaurants around the UK, but this one, in West Street, London, is the original.

title: In 'Rules'
tags: rules restaurant interior staircase steps stairs lookingdown london coventgarden lookdown
description: So to 'Rules' in Covent Garden for a 50th Birthday treat from the In-Laws. One of the oldest and most established restaurant's in the world. Where past clientele hang on the walls in painted portraits and very old copies of 'Punch' magazine alongside the odd stags head mounted on the wall here and there. Not feeling comfortable using my big camera in such a place among the diners I sneaked off to the loo and shot a picture of the staircase on my phone instead.

We both had the plant-based tasting menu:

1)
Beetroot Meringue and Pistachio (back right)
Courgette Flower / Broad Bean
Artichoke / Olive / Datterini (front)

2)
British Heirloom Tomatoes - Basil / Black Olive / Tomato Consommé / Bloody Mary Granita

3)
Jerusalem Artichoke and Black Truffle - Roasted Artichoke / Piedmont Hazelnuts / English Pear /Artichoke Hazelnut Cream

4)
Heritage Carrot - Young Tumip / Cashew Cumin Praline / Coriander

5)
Roasted Broccoli Stem - Seaweed / Meyer Lemon / Toasted Wild Rice / Flaked Almond

"Julienne Bruno" Burella

Kentish Peach / Mint / Lavoche

Italian Green Fig Brûlée - Fig Leaf and Mint Sorbet / Raspberry / Marigold

9)
Ethically Sourced 80% Mayan Red Honduras Cocoa Chocolate Mousse /
Kentish Cherry / Kirsch / Tarragon / "Meringue"

10)
Seabuckthorn Pâte de Fruits / Madeleine / Peanut Butter Rice Crispy

title: Corner of Brick Lane & Swanfield Street, Bethnal Green, London
tags: victorianlondon eastend originalfeatures shop arnoldcircus historiclondon forgottenlondon boundaryestate swanfieldstreet eastlondon londonhistory urbandecay bricklane hiddenlondon london bethnalgreen oldnichol londonarchitecture
description:
This small but remarkable building stands at the very northern tip of Brick Lane, on the corner of Swanfield Street in Bethnal Green, east London, and it has outlasted almost everything that once surrounded it.

The ground floor retains the form of a Victorian shopfront, the kind of modest commercial premises that lined this stretch of Brick Lane throughout the nineteenth century. Cobblers, tailors, chandlers, small food sellers, tradespeople of every kind. Above it, the building shifts register entirely: rendered upper floors, a projecting oriel window with subdivided glazing that has a faintly Arts and Crafts quality. It reads like several decades of London life compressed into one narrow corner plot.

What makes its survival so striking is what used to surround it. This corner sits on the very edge of the Old Nichol, the most notorious slum in Victorian London. A tangle of overcrowded streets where nearly 6,000 people were packed into appalling conditions, the death rate from violent crime ran at twice the Bethnal Green average, and one child in four did not survive their first year. In the 1890s the newly formed London County Council cleared the entire area and built in its place what became Britain's first council estate: the Boundary Estate. The mound at Arnold Circus, just minutes from here, is literally made of rubble from the demolished slum.

Swanfield Street itself was created as part of that clearance. Old Nichol Street was widened, extended and renamed. By the turn of the century, this corner was deep in the heart of London's Jewish East End, where Jewish immigrants formed the overwhelming majority of the population along Brick Lane and the surrounding streets.

This building was standing through all of it. The clearance boundary stopped close enough that it survived while the world around it was razed and rebuilt.

It now sits incongruously between postwar housing estates, a small and stubborn remnant of a vanished city.

title: Shop window, Brick Lane, Bethnal Green
tags: historiclondon urbandecay boundaryestate londonarchitecture originalfeatures oldnichol eastlondon victorianlondon bricklane reddoor hiddenlondon shopfrontdetail london shop swanfieldstreet arnoldcircus bethnalgreen eastend forgottenlondon londonhistory
description:
The final and closest image in this series brings you right up to the glass of a Victorian shop-dwelling on the corner of Swanfield Street and Brick Lane, Bethnal Green. Through wire-reinforced safety glazing, a mid-twentieth century retrofit into the original Victorian shopfront, the interior is just visible: a piece of embroidered or lace fabric hanging on a wire hanger in what would once have been a shop window display, a white panel propped against the glass, the evidence of accumulated domestic life behind a door that no longer seems to open for business.

The wall-mounted light above the door is original, possibly brass, still in place after well over a century. The door is a later replacement, a concession to a more recent era. The wire glass tells of someone, at some point, trying to bring the building up to date.

This building has stood on this corner since at least the Victorian period, on the outer edge of the Old Nichol, London's most notorious slum, cleared in the 1890s to build the Boundary Estate. Very little else from that era survived. This shopfront did, along with its glass floor tiles, its timbers, its red paint, and whatever quiet life continues behind the glass.

title: Red shop, Brick Lane - Victorian Shopfront Detail, Bethnal Green
tags: historiclondon urbandecay boundaryestate londonarchitecture mosaictiles originalfeatures oldnichol victoriantiles eastlondon victorianlondon bricklane reddoor hiddenlondon shopfrontdetail london shop swanfieldstreet arnoldcircus bethnalgreen eastend forgottenlondon londonhistory
description:
A close study of the ground-floor shopfront of a surviving Victorian shop-dwelling on the corner of Brick Lane and Swanfield Street, Bethnal Green.

Up close, the quality and completeness of what remains is striking: the glazed shopfront with its original tongue-and-groove timber surround and curved bay above, a replacement door of a much later date fitted into the Victorian frame, its plain flat panels and aluminium letterbox a practical intrusion into what was once a more considered shopfront, and the original pavement lights still underfoot, thick glass tiles set into the pavement to throw natural light into a basement below.. Through the right-hand window, a piece of lace fabric hangs on a wire, a domestic detail in an otherwise abandoned-feeling space.

This is a building that appears on Victorian-era maps of the area, standing on the outer edge of the Old Nichol slum before it was cleared to create the Boundary Estate in the 1890s. Very little from that streetscape survives.

title: Prologue 014 : Fulford Street and Rotherhithe Street
tags: prologue onerevolutionfortheroad rotherhithe fulfordstreet shop leonardburnikell shopkeeper whitelion torbay kingsarms wheatsheaf thegrapes
description:
Looking over the wall, the 21st century visitor can perhaps yearn to hear the calls of boatmen, barges being unloaded, cranes hoisting up sacks into the warehouses and the general sounds analogous with the old river trade. Today, when the tide is low, one is more likely to encounter mudlarkers unearthing the city's past on the muddy foreshore. On a larger scale, the old warehouses may have gone but there is something quite pleasurable to witness London's palimpsest being scraped back to reveal fragments of the past.

Backtracking slightly to that lone old building facing Fulford Street. This artistic impression shows the old shop that stood on the corner of Fulford Street, the view looking backwards towards The Angel. It is an inter-war view when Leonard Burnikell was the shopkeeper.

The riverside path towards Prince's Stairs actually passes beneath some of the apartment blocks built on wharves served by a trio of taverns - the White Lion, The Torbay, and King's Arms. The lost section of Rotherhithe Street also featured The Wheatsheaf and The Grapes.

Redevelopment and slum clearance started in the 1930s, German bombers took the place of wrecking balls during the blitz, and post-war planners removed the remaining vestiges of this part of old Rotherhithe. It makes the survival of The Angel all the more precious. If a resident of Victorian days could be teleported to the 21st century he or she would ask what had been done to the neighbourhood, wishing perhaps that they had purchased a return ticket. The old streets may have been grimy, and conditions harsh, but they had plenty of soul.

title: Shop - London Transport Museum - Tube map
tags: londontransportmuseum london england unitedkingdom greatbritain greaterlondon cityofwestminster coventgarden tavistockcourt tavistockst londonstransportmuseum transportforlondon gradeiilistedbuilding gradeiilisted formermarkethall williamrogersofcubitts redandwhitebrickwithstoneandterracottadressingsglassroofsitalianatedetailing wellingtonst shop ceiling tubemap
description: A visit to the London Transport Museum. We had to book in advance an Annual Pass, and select at time before going. The London Transport Museum (LTM) is a transport museum based in Covent Garden, London. The museum predominantly hosts exhibits relating to the heritage of London's transport, as well as conserving and explaining the history of it. The majority of the museum's exhibits originated in the collections of London Transport, but, since the creation of Transport for London (TfL) in 2000, the remit of the museum has expanded to cover all aspects of transport in the city and in some instances beyond. The museum operates from two sites within London. The main site in Covent Garden uses the name of its parent institution, and is open to the public every day, excluding over Christmas, having reopened in 2007 after a two-year refurbishment. The other site, located in Acton, is the London Transport Museum Depot and is principally a storage site of historic artefacts that is open to the public on scheduled visitor days throughout the year. The museum also runs a programme of guided tours, Hidden London, which takes visitors to part of the London Underground that are typically closed to the public, including disused stations and wartime shelters. The museum was briefly renamed London's Transport Museum to reflect its coverage of topics beyond London Transport, but it reverted to its previous name in 2007 to coincide with the reopening of the Covent Garden site. In July 2024, the Museum unveiled new branding in an attempt to reflect its coverage of all of London's transport system, not exclusively buses and trains. The new logo - still of roundel design - incorporates all the colours of TfL's transport modes. The museum's main facility is located in a Victorian iron and glass building that had formed part of the Covent Garden vegetable, fruit and flower market. It was designed as a dedicated flower market by William Rogers in 1871 and is located between Russell Street, Tavistock Street, Wellington Street and the east side of the former market square. The market moved out in 1971, and the building was reopened as the London Transport Museum on 28 March 1980. The collection had been located at Syon Park since 1973 and before that had formed part of the British Transport Museum at Clapham. On 4 September 2005 the museum closed for a 拢22 million refurbishment designed by Bryan Avery of Avery Associates Architects to enable the expansion of the display collection to encompass the larger remit of TfL which administers all forms of public transport. Enhanced educational facilities were also required. The museum reopened on 22 November 2007. The entrance to the museum is from the Covent Garden Piazza. The museum is within walking distance from both Covent Garden Underground station and Charing Cross railway station. Grade II listed building London Transport Museum Shop - Tube map

tags: camden carrying chimneypot dusk england europe irishpub kentishtown kentishtownroad london pizzabox quinnspub quinns streetscene terracedbuilding traditionalenglishpub uk unitedkingdom urban victorianarchitecture weatheredbuilding airplane architecture authentic blue bollard building carryingpizza chimney city citylife conveniencestore evening facade graffiti gray intersection man neonsign oldbuilding outdoorseating pedestrian peelingpaint people pub reallife road rooftop shop sidewalk sitting street streetcorner streetsign tree twilight walking weathered window yellow grunge
description:
Irish pub, Kentish Town Road, London, England, United Kingdom, UK
On a summer evening in London's Kentish Town, the historic Quinn's pub provides a warm, inviting glow at a street corner on Kentish Town Road. As dusk settles over the city in July, daily life continues around it: pedestrians cross the street, a man waits with takeaway food, and patrons relax at outdoor tables. The scene captures a typical moment, contrasting the building's weathered character with the ongoing rhythm of urban life.

description: Stoke Newington, Hackney, London. Originally The Apollo Picture House built in 1913 it went through various name changes and closed as a cinema in 1983. It was bought by the UK Turkish Islamic Association and, eventually, converted into a mosque, restaurant, butchers shop and school. It is unique in that services are conducted in Turkish as opposed to the usual Arabic or English.

title: London - St Anne's Church, Soho
tags: london england architecture urban city streetscene shop shopfront shopfronts shops
description: This location has the strangest of histories. Built in the mid 17th century it was bombed in 1940. The surviving tower of the church is an architectural oddity. Visitors can climb to see the bell (dating from 1686). From its entrance on Dean St the parish does work for the homeless and drug addicts. This view is of the south entrance (now a souvenir shop) on Shaftesbury Ave, Soho.

description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd Another upload from last months London Flickr Group Photowalk, this is the appropriately named Plumage House in Islington. I believe H. Bestimt & Co Feather Merchants closed about ten years ago but for quite a while they were the leading supplier of exotic feathers to the BBC, West End theatres etc for use on period clothing. From a distance, it was the verdigris colour that caught my eye but as I got closer it became apparent that very little, if any, of the facade is actually made from copper. title: David Kira / Fournier Street.
tags: 2024 england fournierstreet london lumixgx9 mft microfourthirds panasonicgx9 panasoniclumixdcgx9 panasoniclumixg14mmf25 spitalfields davidkira bananas shop vintagefrontage history bananamerchant lamp sign
description: I have often walked past this shopfront, in Spitalfields. Never really paying much attention, thinking it was some trendy architect or such like. If only I had sooner noticed the writing to the left and right of the name. 'Bananas and Tomatoes' and 'Salesman', they read. I went off to find out more and unearthed some interesting history, in the link below. I am familiar with this website. It's run by The Gentle Author and Spitalfields Life is a fantastic resource for past and present anecdotes of the East End of London and further afield. When I've finished one article..I just keep going. He is a most entertaining writer. Spitalfields has been home to many immigrants and a renowned market. Although it has been somewhat gentrified now, it still has a variety of stories to tell. spitalfieldslife.com/2011/12/30/david-kira-ltd-banana-mer... Taken with my Panasonic Lumix GX9 camera and a Panasonic G 14mm f/2.5 lens.

title: UK - London Flickr Group Photowalk 17 - Fonthill Rd - Mannequin_5004625
tags: uklondonflickrgroupphotowalk17fonthillrdmannequin5004625 mannequin dummy fonthillroad fonthillrd finsburypark london fashion shop londonflickrphotowalk londonflickrphotowalk17
description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd A mannequin in the window of one of over 100 dress shops on the Fonthill Road, just round the corner from Finbury Park tube station. One of the best things about the London Flickr Group Photowalks is that my fellow Admin @Fozelek has a greater knowledge of some of the more off the beaten track bits of the city and therefore I keep discovering new bits of the city. The vibrant Fonthill Road is a good example of this and somewhere I'll head back to explore more soon. Our next Photowalk is scheduled for Saturday April 20th, more details here if you're interesting in joining us for this free event : www.flickr.com/groups/londonflickrgroup/discuss/721577219... Click here for more photos from London Flickr Group Photowalks : www.flickr.com/photos/darrellg/albums/72177720301569918 From Wikipedia, "The 1960s and 1970s saw a large influx of Bangladeshis alongside pockets of Pakistanis, Indians and Burmese who settled in the borough. Many moved to surrounding locales as their economic prowess grew in the 1980s. They made significant contributions to local business and politics as well as to religious institutions (e.g. the founding of the original Finsbury Park Mosque). A number of immigrant-led businesses opened on Blackstock Road, an area associated with the Algerian immigrant community amongst others. There had been a large minority of the African Caribbean diaspora arriving in the area bordering Manor House throughout the same period and as early as the Windrush generation. Greek Cypriots and later Turkish Cypriots started to arrive in the 1960s and 70s as economic migrants firmly establishing themselves in business through the clothing trade on Fonthill Road. In the 1980s and 90s, immigrants included significant populations of Somalis populating the area as refugees and asylum seekers at the height of the crisis in their homeland and more recent arrivals of settled EU nationals from Scandinavian countries." 漏 D.Godliman

title: UK - London - Oxford St - H and M - Double Denim_5004552
tags: uklondonoxfordsthandmdoubledenim5004552 oxfordcircus hm shop shopping oxfordstreet oxfordst london hmhennesmauritzab hmgroup clothingcompany fastfashion retailer business erlingpersson hennes clothing store mauritzwidforss hennesmauritz 漏dgodliman advert shopwindow model denim passerby
description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd Taken during my most recent trip to London, I'm always on the lookout for supersized figures in shop windows as they can create some interesting juxtapositions such as this one at the Oxford Circus H&M. Click here for more of my Street photogrphy : www.flickr.com/photos/darrellg/albums/72157629075346606 From Wikipedia, "H & M Hennes & Mauritz AB, also known as H&M Group, is a multinational clothing company based in Sweden that focuses on fast-fashion clothing. As of 23 June 2022, H&M Group operated in 75 geographical markets with 4,801 stores under the various company brands, with 107,375 full-time equivalent positions. H&M is the second-largest international clothing retailer after Inditex. H&M was founded by Erling Persson in 1947 under the name Hennes. The company's current CEO is Daniel Erver who replaced former CEO Helena Helmersson in January 2024. The business was established in 1947 by Erling Persson when he opened his first shop in V盲ster氓s, Sweden. The shop was called Hennes (Swedish for 'hers') and only sold women's clothing. Another store opened in Norway in 1964.[16] In 1968, Persson acquired the hunting apparel retailer Mauritz Widforss in Stockholm, which led to the inclusion of a menswear collection in the product range, and the name was changed to Hennes & Mauritz." 漏 D.Godliman

The business was established in 1947 by Erling Persson when he opened his first shop in V盲ster氓s, Sweden. The shop was called Hennes (Swedish for 'hers') and only sold women's clothing. Another store opened in Norway in 1964.[16] In 1968, Persson acquired the hunting apparel retailer Mauritz Widforss in Stockholm, which led to the inclusion of a menswear collection in the product range, and the name was changed to Hennes & Mauritz." 漏 D.Godliman

title: London - Oxford Street - Latest News 2 -13 January 2024
tags: london oxfordstreet lbc hm shop store shopping news postoffice scandal fujitsu horizon 2024
description:
Oxford Street in London. The video screen provides adverts and news updates from LBC - a London radio station. The story about Paula Vennells, concerns the 'Post Office Scandal', which has been described as the widest miscarriage of justice in British History.

Hundreds of Post Masters were wrongly prosecuted for crimes of theft, fraud and false accounting that they did not commit. Some went to jail while others were bankrupted. Families broke up under the stress and some committed suicide.

The Post Masters were victims of a faulty computer based accounting system called Horizon - developed by Fujitsu. Horizon had bugs that erroneously showed that some Post Masters were stealing money. 

However, it was not simply a defective accounting programme that led to prosecutions and wrongful convictions.  The Post Office and Fujitsu lied about known issues with Horizon.  Innocent peoples lives were destroyed because of those lies.

The woman on the screen is Paula Vennells. She is a former  Chief Executive Officer of Post Office Limited. She was the 'Post Office Boss', during part of the period when the false prosecutions were taking place.  It has become clear that she, along with others at the Post Office and Fujitsu, deliberately took measures to conceal the problems with Horizon. 

Some of the victims, of the 'Post Office Scandal' are now suggesting that those who took part in the conspiracy that wrecked their lives, should face criminal charges themselves.

Since the 18th century the street has housed many prestigious and upmarket fashion retailers. The southern section is Old Bond Street and the longer northern section New Bond Street鈥攁 distinction not generally made in everyday usage. The street was built on fields surrounding Clarendon House on Piccadilly, which were developed by Sir Thomas Bond. It was built up in the 1720s, and by the end of the 18th century was a popular place for the upper-class residents of Mayfair to socialise. Prestigious or expensive shops were established along the street, but it declined as a centre of social activity in the 19th century, although it held its reputation as a fashionable place for retail, and is home to the auction houses Sotheby's and Bonhams (formerly Phillips) and the department store Fenwick and jeweller Tiffany's. title: UK - London - Fortnum and Mason - Peacock facade sq_5009283
tags: uklondonfortnumandmasonpeacockfacadesq5009283 fortnummason fortnumandmason picadilly london facade peacock sculpture publicart decoration shop store departmentstore building
description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd A fabulour peacock sculpture decorating the front of the famous Fortnum & Mason store on Picadilly in London. Think this was probably in aid of King Charles III's Coronation which was due two days after I took this photo. Click here for more 'shopping' themed shots : www.flickr.com/photos/darrellg/albums/72157606211569085 From Wikipedia, "William Fortnum was a footman in the household of Queen Anne. The royal family's insistence on having new candles every night resulted in large amounts of half-used wax, which Fortnum promptly resold. Fortnum also had a side business as a grocer. He convinced his landlord, Hugh Mason, to be his associate, and they founded the first Fortnum & Mason store in Mason's small shop in St James's Market in 1707. In 1761, William Fortnum's grandson Charles went into the service of Queen Charlotte, and the connection with the royal court led to an increase in business. Fortnum & Mason claims to have invented the Scotch egg, in 1738. The store began to stock speciality items, namely ready-to-eat luxury meals such as poultry or game served in aspic jelly. During the Napoleonic Wars, the emporium supplied dried fruit, spices and other preserves to British officers and during the Victorian era it was frequently called upon to provide food for prestigious court functions. Queen Victoria sent shipments of Fortnum & Mason's concentrated beef tea to Florence Nightingale's hospitals during the Crimean War." 漏 D.Godliman

title: UK - London - Old Bond St - Hoarding Gucci_5002230
tags: uklondonoldbondsthoardinggucci5002230 gucci brand hoarding shop oldbondst bondst london streetphotography streetphotographer blue nikond500 nikon
description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd The Old Bond St Gucci store hoarding remains in place so it seemed rude not to take another shot when I was in London for the last London Flickr Group photowalk. Our next photowalk is next Saturday, 23rd September if you're interested in coming along : www.flickr.com/groups/londonflickrgroup/discuss/721577219... Click here for more 'shopping' themed shots : www.flickr.com/photos/darrellg/albums/72157606211569085 From Wikipedia, "Gucci is an Italian high-end luxury fashion house based in Florence, Italy. Its product lines include handbags, ready-to-wear, footwear, accessories, and home decoration; and it licenses its name and branding to Coty, Inc. for fragrance and cosmetics under the name Gucci Beauty. Gucci was founded in 1921 by Guccio Gucci (1881鈥?953) in Florence, Tuscany. Under the direction of Aldo Gucci (son of Guccio), Gucci became a worldwide-known brand, an icon of the Italian Dolce Vita period. Following family feuds during the 1980s, the Gucci family was entirely ousted from the capital of the company by 1993. After this crisis, the brand was revived with a provocative 'Porno Chic' props. In 1999, Gucci was acquired by the French conglomerate Pinault Printemps Redoute, which later became Kering. During the 2010s, Gucci became an iconic 'geek-chic' brand." 漏 D.Godliman

tags: uklondonoxfordstundercoveragent5002229 agentprovocateur lingerie underwear shop brand bondst sale shopping shopwindow windowshopping
description: My Website : Twitter : Facebook : Instagram : Photocrowd The Agent Provocateur shop, just off Bond St, phoographed when I was in London for the last months London Flickr Group photowalk. Our next photowalk is this Saturday, 23rd September if you're interested in coming along : www.flickr.com/groups/londonflickrgroup/discuss/721577219... Click here for more 'shopping' themed shots : www.flickr.com/photos/darrellg/albums/72157606211569085 From Wikipedia, "In 1994, Agent Provocateur was founded by Joseph Corr茅, the son of Vivienne Westwood, and his then-wife Serena Rees. The first store opened in Soho on Broadwick Street. The company sells colourful and fashionable lingerie. Joseph Corr茅 is the designer for the brand. In 2007, after the divorce of Corr茅 and Rees, 3i, a private equity firm that invests in mid-size companies, purchased 80% of the company for 拢60 million. Between November 2007 and March 2009 the company opened 13 shops, expanding to the US, Russia, Dubai and Hong Kong By March 2008, Agent Provocateur's profits dropped 18% to 拢2.2 million due to the cost of expansion. Garry Hogarth stepped down as CEO in February 2016. In March 2017, the business entered administration. As part of a "pre-pack" deal, it was purchased by Four Holdings, a company one-third owned by British businessman Mike Ashley who reportedly paid around "拢25m after seeing off competition from private equity firm Lion Capital." 漏 D.Godliman
